schools.summary: Gill: the command was to go up, not from the place where Moses was, the top of the mount, but where the people were, to depart toward Canaan; the witnesses note God commanding Moses and the people to depart and go up to the land sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the renewing of the march toward the promised land, the oath to the fathers held fast (despite the calf, God still owning his oath and sending them on: 'Depart, and go up hence... unto the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob'; the command, 'Depart, and go up hence,' that they should set forward from Sinai toward Canaan; the manner of the speech, 'the people which thou hast brought up out of Egypt,' that God here speaks of them as Moses's people, thou hast brought up, in a kind of distance after their great sin; the ground of the journey, 'the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob,' that the promise still stood on the oath to the fathers, Unto thy seed will I give it; the comfort, that, though the people had sinned grievously, God held fast his ancient covenant and oath, for his promise fails not; that the faithfulness of God to his oath, not the worthiness of the people, carried them on).

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Depart, and go up hence
unto the land which I sware unto Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, Unto thy seed will I give it
God bidding Moses and the people depart for the land sworn to the fathers, despite the calf
the faithfulness of God to his oath, not the worthiness of the people, carrying them on

schools.summary: Gill: the angel here was not the Angel before promised, the Angel of his presence, the eternal Word and Son of God, but a created angel; the witnesses note God promising to send an angel before Israel to drive out the Canaanite and the other nations — the offer of a created angel in place of God's own presence, a lesser favour after their sin (God would send an angel, but not himself go up: 'I will send an angel before thee; and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite... the Jebusite'; the gift, 'I will send an angel before thee,' but, as the commentators mark, not the Angel before promised, the Angel of his presence, the eternal Word and Son of God, but a created angel; the contrast, that earlier God had promised the Angel in whom his name was, but now, after the calf, offers only a created angel, a lesser thing; the work promised, 'I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite,' that the nations would still be driven out before them; the meaning the commentators draw, that God would still give them the land and a guardian, but the threatened loss of his own immediate presence was the sore part, a token of his displeasure; that to have all the gifts without the Giver's presence is a poor thing).

debates.question: What 'angel' does God now offer to send before Israel?

sides.position: Not the Angel of his presence (the eternal Word and Son of God) promised earlier, but a created angel — a lesser favour after the calf, the withholding of God's own immediate presence being the sore part, a token of his displeasure

themes: And I will send an angel before thee
and I will drive out the Canaanite, the Amorite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite
God offering a created angel, not the Angel of his presence, to drive out the nations
a lesser favour after the calf, the loss of his own presence the sore part; the gifts without the Giver a poor thing

schools.summary: Gill: the land flowing with milk and honey, abounding with all the good things of life, a description of Canaan; but God would not go up in the midst of them; the witnesses note God promising the good land, but declaring he would not go up in their midst, for they were stiffnecked, lest he consume them — the sore reason for withholding his presence, the danger of the holy God dwelling among a sinful people (his very presence might destroy them: 'I will not go up in the midst of thee; for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way'; the land, 'a land flowing with milk and honey,' Canaan, abounding with all the necessaries and good things of life; the refusal, 'I will not go up in the midst of thee,' that God would not go among them as before, in his immediate presence; the reason, 'for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way,' that, being so prone to sin and provoking, the holy God dwelling among them might break out in just wrath and consume them; the solemn truth the commentators draw, that the nearer presence of the holy God to a sinful people is the more dangerous, his holiness a consuming fire to sin; that it is mercy as well as judgment that God withdraws, sparing them; that only through a Mediator can the holy God safely dwell with sinners).

themes: Unto a land flowing with milk and honey: for I will not go up in the midst of thee
for thou art a stiffnecked people: lest I consume thee in the way
God promising the good land but refusing to go in their midst, lest his holiness consume the stiffnecked people
his holiness a consuming fire to sin; only through a Mediator can the holy God safely dwell with sinners

schools.summary: Gill: the evil tidings were that God would withdraw his gracious presence and not go up with them himself, only sending an angel; at this they mourned; the witnesses note the people, hearing these evil tidings, mourning and putting off their ornaments — the people's grief at the threatened loss of God's presence, a hopeful sign of repentance (the worst news the withdrawing of God himself: 'when the people heard these evil tidings, they mourned: and no man did put on him his ornaments'; the tidings, 'these evil tidings,' namely, that God would withdraw his gracious presence, and go not up with them himself, only send an angel; the grief, 'they mourned,' that the people were grieved and cast down at this word, the loss of God's presence accounted the heaviest of evils; the token of mourning, 'no man did put on him his ornaments,' that they laid aside their finery, in humiliation and sorrow, as mourners; the hopeful sign the commentators draw, that the people's grief was a token of true sorrow and beginning repentance, that they so felt the loss of God; that to a gracious heart the withdrawing of God's presence is the sorest of all losses; that the loss of God is felt by those who love him above all gifts).

themes: And when the people heard these evil tidings, they mourned
and no man did put on him his ornaments
the people mourning and putting off their ornaments at the threatened loss of God's presence
the loss of God the heaviest of evils, a hopeful sign of repentance, the sorest loss to a gracious heart

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the LORD bade Israel put off their ornaments, as a sign of humiliation, that he might consider how to deal with them; the witnesses note God charging Israel, as a stiffnecked people whom his presence might consume, to put off their ornaments, that he might know what to do with them — the call to humiliation, that mercy might find a way (they must humble themselves and lay aside their pride: 'Ye are a stiffnecked people... therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee, that I may know what to do unto thee'; the charge, 'Ye are a stiffnecked people,' the same hard truth pressed home, the cause of all the danger; the peril, 'I will come up into the midst of thee in a moment, and consume thee,' that his holy presence, among such a people, might in a moment break out and destroy them; the command, 'put off thy ornaments from thee,' that they should lay aside their finery, a sign of humiliation, abasing themselves before God; the gracious purpose, 'that I may know what to do unto thee,' spoken after the manner of men, that God, seeing their humiliation, might consider how to deal with them, leaving room for mercy on their repentance; that God calls the sinner to humble himself, that mercy may find a way; that humiliation goes before grace).

themes: Ye are a stiffnecked people: I will come up into the midst of thee in a moment, and consume thee
therefore now put off thy ornaments from thee, that I may know what to do unto thee
God bidding the stiffnecked people put off their ornaments in humiliation, that mercy might find a way
God calling the sinner to humble himself, leaving room for mercy on repentance, humiliation going before grace

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments from Mount Horeb onward, putting them off as a lasting token of penitence; the witnesses note the children of Israel stripping themselves of their ornaments by Mount Horeb — the people's obedience in humiliation, the outward token of their penitence (they did as God bade, laying aside their finery: 'the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments by the mount Horeb'; the act, 'stripped themselves of their ornaments,' that they obeyed the command, putting off such as before described, the finery they had worn; the place and time, 'by the mount Horeb,' there at Sinai, and from Mount Horeb onward, as a lasting token; the meaning the commentators draw, that this stripping was the outward sign of inward humiliation and penitence, the people abasing themselves before the God they had offended; that true repentance shows itself in humbling acts, the laying aside of pride and ornament; that the people who had so lately decked themselves and sinned now stripped themselves and mourned; that humiliation is the fit posture of a people that has provoked God).

themes: And the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments
by the mount Horeb
Israel stripping off their ornaments by Mount Horeb in obedient humiliation
the outward token of inward penitence, true repentance showing itself in humbling acts

schools.summary: Gill: this was not the great tabernacle, the pattern of which had been shown, for that was not yet made, but Moses' own tent, or one erected for worship while the great one was building; the witnesses note Moses taking the tabernacle and pitching it far outside the camp, where all who sought the LORD went out to it — the removing of the place of meeting outside the camp, the distance sin had made (the meeting-place set afar off: 'Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp... every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle... which was without the camp'; the tent, 'the tabernacle,' not the great tabernacle, not yet built, but his own tabernacle or tent, or one erected for worship in the meantime; the place, 'without the camp, afar off from the camp,' that, after the calf, the place of meeting with God was removed outside the camp, a token that sin had put a distance between God and the people; the seekers, 'every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle,' that those who truly sought God had to go forth, outside the polluted camp, to seek him; the meaning the commentators draw, that sin separates from God and removes his presence, and that those who would find him must go forth to seek him, even bearing reproach; that the way to God was now outside the camp, a place of separation).

themes: Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp, afar off from the camp
every one which sought the LORD went out unto the tabernacle of the congregation, which was without the camp
Moses pitching the tent of meeting outside the camp, a token of the distance sin had made
sin separating from God, those who would find him going forth to seek him outside the camp, even bearing reproach

schools.summary: Gill: when Moses had pitched it he did not continue there, but went to and fro, to meet the Lord and to transact the affairs of the people, especially the great affair of their reconciliation; the witnesses note the people rising and standing at their tent doors, looking after Moses as he went out to the tabernacle — the reverent regard of the people for their mediator going to God (they watch Moses go in to plead for them: 'when Moses went out unto the tabernacle... all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door, and looked after Moses'; the going forth, 'when Moses went out unto the tabernacle,' that Moses went to and fro, not dwelling there but going to meet the Lord and especially the great affair of the people's reconciliation; the people's posture, 'all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door,' that, in reverence, they all rose and stood, watching; the looking, 'looked after Moses, until he was gone into the tabernacle,' that they followed him with their eyes till he entered, anxious for the outcome of his pleading; the meaning the commentators draw, that the people, conscious of their sin and danger, looked to their mediator with reverence and hope, knowing all depended on his meeting with God; the type, that as Israel looked after Moses, so sinners look to Christ, the great Mediator, gone in to God for them; that all our hope hangs on our Mediator's going in to God).

themes: when Moses went out unto the tabernacle, all the people rose up, and stood every man at his tent door
and looked after Moses, until he was gone into the tabernacle
the people reverently watching their mediator go in to plead for them
as Israel looked after Moses, so sinners look to Christ the great Mediator, gone in to God for them

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the cloud descending at these times, and at none others, precluded the possibility of deception, a full proof of the divine presence; the witnesses note that, as Moses entered the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended and stood at the door, and the LORD talked with him — the visible token of God's presence and his speaking with Moses (the cloud coming down where the mediator enters, and God speaks: 'as Moses entered into the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended, and stood at the door of the tabernacle, and the LORD talked with Moses'; the descent, 'the cloudy pillar descended,' coming down from the top of the mount in which Jehovah was, the visible sign of God's presence; the standing, 'stood at the door of the tabernacle,' that it rested at the door where Moses was, marking the place of meeting; the proof Clarke draws, that the cloud descending at these times, and at none others, was a full proof, precluding the possibility of deception, that God indeed met with Moses; the speaking, 'the LORD talked with Moses,' that God conversed with him there; the meaning, that God answers the mediator who goes in for the people, coming down to meet and speak with him; the comfort, that, though removed from the camp, God still met with Moses for the people's sake; that God hears and answers the intercessor).

themes: as Moses entered into the tabernacle, the cloudy pillar descended, and stood at the door of the tabernacle
and the LORD talked with Moses
the cloudy pillar descending as the LORD talked with Moses, the token of his presence
the cloud at these times and none others a proof against deception; God answering the mediator who goes in for the people

schools.summary: John Wesley: when they saw the cloudy pillar, they worshipped, thereby owning God's presence with Moses and giving him their hearts and reverence; the witnesses note all the people, seeing the cloudy pillar stand at the door, rising up and worshipping at their tent doors — the people's worship at the sight of God's presence, a hopeful token of mercy (they bow at the sign of God still near: 'all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door: and all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door'; the sight, 'all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door,' the visible token that God was still meeting with Moses; the comfort, that this was a pleasing sight to them, giving some hope that God would be merciful to them, forgive their sin, and not depart from them; the worship, 'all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door,' that, owning God's presence with Moses, they bowed in reverence and adoration; the meaning the commentators draw, that the sight of God's presence draws forth worship, and that the people, hoping in his mercy through the mediator, worshipped; that those who hope in God's mercy through the Mediator worship him; that the token of God's nearness is a call to worship).

themes: all the people saw the cloudy pillar stand at the tabernacle door
and all the people rose up and worshipped, every man in his tent door
the people rising to worship at the sight of God's presence in the cloudy pillar
a pleasing sight that gave hope of mercy; the sight of God's presence drawing forth worship from those who hope through the Mediator

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake to Moses not by an angel but himself in person, not by dream or vision but apparently, in real visible appearance, clearly, mouth to mouth; the witnesses note the LORD speaking with Moses face to face, as a man with his friend, Moses returning to the camp but Joshua not departing from the tabernacle — the high privilege of Moses' communion with God, and the faithful waiting of Joshua (God speaking with Moses as a friend, plainly and intimately: 'the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend'; the manner, 'face to face... as a man speaketh unto his friend,' not by an angel, but he himself in person, not by a dream or vision, but apparently, clearly, mouth to mouth, with the openness and intimacy of friendship; the rare honour, that this was a privilege above the prophets, who had dreams and dark speeches, Moses speaking with God as a friend; the type some draw, that this intimate communion shadows the fellowship believers have with God in Christ, who calls them friends; Moses' return, 'he turned again into the camp,' going back to the people with God's word; Joshua's waiting, 'his servant Joshua... departed not out of the tabernacle,' that the young man kept by the place of God's presence, faithful and waiting).

debates.question: What was the privilege of God speaking with Moses 'face to face, as a man... unto his friend'?

sides.position: A privilege above the prophets — not by dream or vision or dark speeches (Numbers 12:6-8), but in person, plainly, mouth to mouth, with the intimacy of friendship; some seeing it shadow the fellowship believers have with God in Christ, who calls them friends (John 15:15)

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend
but his servant Joshua, the son of Nun, a young man, departed not out of the tabernacle
the LORD speaking with Moses face to face as a friend, plainly, above the prophets; Joshua keeping faithfully by the tabernacle
some seeing it shadow the fellowship believers have with God in Christ, who calls them friends

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Moses, returned to the door of the tabernacle, becomes a humble and importunate supplicant for two great favours, and as a prince has power with God and prevails; the witnesses note Moses laying his case before God, that he was bidden to bring up the people but not told who should go with him, pleading God's word that he knew him by name and he had found grace — the opening of Moses' intercession, pleading God's own promises (he urges God's words back upon him: 'thou sayest unto me, Bring up this people: and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me. Yet thou hast said, I know thee by name'; Moses' boldness, that, as a humble and importunate supplicant, he as a prince has power with God and prevails; the case, 'thou sayest... Bring up this people... and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me,' that the charge was laid on him without the promised presence; the plea, 'thou hast said, I know thee by name, and thou hast also found grace in my sight,' that Moses pleads God's own gracious words, his being known by name and his finding grace, as the ground of his asking; the meaning the commentators draw, that the way to prevail with God is to plead his own words and promises back to him; that God loves to be held to his word by an importunate faith).

themes: thou sayest unto me, Bring up this people: and thou hast not let me know whom thou wilt send with me
Yet thou hast said, I know thee by name, and thou hast also found grace in my sight
Moses opening his intercession, pleading God's own words that he knew him by name and he had found grace
the way to prevail with God being to plead his own promises back to him, an importunate faith holding God to his word

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses prayed, if he had found grace, that God would show him his way, that he might know him and continue to find grace, and that God would consider the nation his people; the witnesses note Moses, on the ground of grace found, praying to be shown God's way, that he might know him and find grace, and pleading that this nation is God's people — the prayer to know God and the plea for the people (Moses asking to know God's ways and owning the people as God's: 'shew me now thy way, that I may know thee... and consider that this nation is thy people'; the ground, 'if I have found grace in thy sight,' which Moses says not as doubting but as taking it for granted and so argues from it, improving his interest in grace; the request, 'shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace,' that Moses desired to know God's way and mind, to know him more, and so continue in his favour; the great longing, that to know God is the height of the believer's desire, and the way to grow in grace; the plea for the people, 'consider that this nation is thy people,' that Moses, in his own asking, does not forget the people, but pleads that they are God's own, not to be cast off; that the true intercessor pleads for the people as God's).

themes: shew me now thy way, that I may know thee, that I may find grace in thy sight
and consider that this nation is thy people
Moses praying to be shown God's way that he might know him, and pleading the nation as God's people
to know God the height of the believer's desire; the true intercessor pleading that the people are God's own

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the Hebrew, 'my faces shall go,' that God would give manifestations of his grace and goodness through the whole journey, varying his appearances as their need required; the witnesses note God answering that his presence should go with Moses and he would give him rest — the gracious granting of the very thing most desired, God's own presence (God promising to go himself and give rest: 'My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest'; the promise, 'My presence shall go with thee,' in the Hebrew my faces shall go, that God himself, not a created angel, would go, giving manifestations of his grace and goodness through the whole journey, varying his appearances as their necessities required; the rest, 'I will give thee rest,' both the rest of Canaan at the journey's end and the rest of soul that God's presence gives by the way; the great mercy the commentators draw, that God, entreated, grants his own presence, the very favour withheld after the calf, restoring what sin had forfeited; that God's presence is the soul's true rest, and that where he goes there is rest; that the answer of prayer is often more than was asked).

debates.question: What does God grant in 'My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest'?

sides.position: His own presence — 'my faces shall go,' God himself, not a created angel, giving manifestations of his grace through the journey — and rest, both the rest of Canaan and the rest of soul his presence gives; restoring the very favour withheld after the calf, his presence the soul's true rest

themes: And he said, My presence shall go with thee
and I will give thee rest
God granting the very thing most desired, his own presence ('my faces shall go') and rest
God restoring what sin had forfeited, his presence the soul's true rest, both Canaan and the rest of soul

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the Hebrew, 'if thy faces do not go,' that without supernatural assistance and the manifestations of God's peculiar providence and grace, they should not be carried up; the witnesses note Moses answering that, if God's presence went not with them, he would not have them carried up — the heart that prefers God's presence to all the gifts of the land (without God's presence Moses will not stir: 'If thy presence go not with me, carry us not up hence'; the resolve, 'if thy presence go not with me,' in the Hebrew if thy faces do not go, that without the manifestations of God's peculiar providence and grace, and supernatural assistance, Moses would not go on; the refusal, 'carry us not up hence,' that he would rather stay in the wilderness with God than go to Canaan without him; the great lesson the commentators draw, that the believer values God's presence above all his gifts, and would rather have God in the desert than the land without him; that the presence of God is better than the promised land, the Giver better than the gift; that a gracious soul cannot be content with anything short of God himself).

debates.question: What does Moses' 'if thy presence go not with me, carry us not up hence' show?

sides.position: That the believer values God's presence above all his gifts — preferring God in the wilderness to Canaan without him; the presence of God better than the promised land, the Giver better than the gift, a gracious soul content with nothing short of God himself

themes: And he said unto him, If thy presence go not with me
carry us not up hence
Moses refusing to be carried up without God's presence, preferring God in the wilderness to Canaan without him
the believer valuing God's presence above all gifts, the Giver better than the gift, content with nothing short of God

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: by this divine protection they should be saved from idolatry and preserved in the true worship of God, and so be separated from all the people on earth; the witnesses note Moses pleading that nothing but God's going with them could show that they had found grace, and that this alone separated them from all other peoples — God's presence the one mark and proof of his special favour (his going with them what distinguishes them: 'wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace... is it not in that thou goest with us? so shall we be separated... from all the people that are upon the face of the earth'; the question, 'wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace,' that there could be no other token of God's special favour; the answer, 'is it not in that thou goest with us?' that God's going with them was the one proof and mark of grace; the separation, 'so shall we be separated... from all the people... upon the face of the earth,' that, by God's presence and protection, they should be saved from idolatry, preserved in the true worship, and so set apart from all the nations; the great truth the commentators draw, that the presence of God among his people is the one thing that marks them his and distinguishes them from the world; that what makes God's people peculiar is his dwelling among them).

debates.question: What is the one mark that God's people have found grace and are separated from all others?

sides.position: God's going with them — his presence among them the one proof of his special favour, and the very thing that, saving them from idolatry and keeping them in the true worship, separates them from all the peoples of the earth, marking them his

themes: wherein shall it be known... that I and thy people have found grace in thy sight? is it not in that thou goest with us?
so shall we be separated, I and thy people, from all the people that are upon the face of the earth
God's presence the one proof of grace and what separates his people from all others
the presence of God among his people the one thing that marks them his and distinguishes them from the world

schools.summary: Gill: God would do this thing also that Moses asked, namely, go with them himself in that amazing and distinguished manner, in the pillar of cloud and fire; the witnesses note God answering that he would do the thing Moses had asked, for he had found grace and God knew him by name — the full granting of the intercession, God yielding to the importunate plea (God granting all Moses asked, on the ground of grace: 'I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken: for thou hast found grace in my sight, and I know thee by name'; the grant, 'I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken,' that God would go with them himself in that amazing and distinguished manner, in the pillar of cloud and fire, granting the very presence asked; the 'also,' that this was a further favour beyond the former, God granting more upon being further entreated; the ground, 'for thou hast found grace in my sight, and I know thee by name,' that God grants the request on the ground of Moses' having found grace and being known by name, the same plea Moses had urged; the great encouragement the commentators draw, that importunate intercession prevails with God, and that he answers the prayer of him whom he favours; that God yields to the pleading of his servants, granting the desire of them that fear him).

themes: I will do this thing also that thou hast spoken: for thou hast found grace in my sight
and I know thee by name
God granting all Moses asked, to go with them himself, on the ground of grace found and being known by name
importunate intercession prevailing with God, who answers the prayer of him whom he favours

schools.summary: Gill: this was not any visible lustre and brightness Moses had seen, nor the glorious essence of God, which is invisible and cannot be seen, but a fuller discovery of God's grace and goodness; the witnesses note Moses, emboldened by the granting of his former requests, beseeching God to show him his glory — the rising of holy desire to a yet higher favour, the sight of God's glory (having gained so much, Moses asks the highest of all: 'I beseech thee, shew me thy glory'; the request, 'shew me thy glory,' not any visible lustre as a symbol of the divine Presence he had already seen, nor the glorious essence of God, which is invisible and cannot be seen, of which Moses could not be ignorant; that what he sought was a fuller and clearer discovery of God's grace and goodness, the brightest manifestation his soul could bear; the holy boldness, that, having found grace and gained his suit, Moses presses on to ask the greatest favour, to see God's glory; the lesson the commentators draw, that the more a soul knows of God, the more it longs to know, holy desire ever reaching higher; that to see God's glory is the height of the soul's longing; that grace makes the soul ambitious of more of God).

themes: And he said, I beseech thee
shew me thy glory
Moses beseeching God to show him his glory, a fuller discovery of his grace and goodness
the more a soul knows of God the more it longs to know, holy desire ever reaching higher

schools.summary: Gill: the glory of the Lord lies in his goodness, which appears in the works of his hands and the methods of his providence, especially in the distribution of his sovereign grace and mercy, and particularly in his pardoning grace; the witnesses note God answering that he would make all his goodness pass before Moses, proclaim his name, and be gracious and merciful to whom he would — the great answer, that God's glory is his goodness, displayed in sovereign grace (God showing his glory by his goodness and free mercy: 'I will make all my goodness pass before thee, and I will proclaim the name of the LORD... and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious'; that the glory of the LORD lies in his goodness, and so, to show his glory, God would make all his goodness pass before Moses; the proclaiming, 'I will proclaim the name of the LORD before thee,' that God would declare his name and nature, his attributes, chiefly his grace and mercy; the sovereignty, 'will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy,' that God's grace and mercy are free and sovereign, given of his own will to whom he pleases, none having a claim; the great truth the commentators draw, that God's glory shines most in his goodness and free grace, and that his mercy is sovereign, owing nothing to any; that the chiefest glory of God is his pardoning grace).

debates.question: In what does God's glory chiefly consist, and how is his mercy bestowed?

sides.position: God's glory lies in his goodness — displayed in his works, providence, and especially his sovereign grace and pardoning mercy; and his grace and mercy are free and sovereign, 'gracious to whom I will be gracious' (Romans 9:15), given of his own will to whom he pleases, none having a claim

themes: I will make all my goodness pass before thee, and I will proclaim the name of the LORD before thee
and will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and will shew mercy on whom I will shew mercy
God showing his glory by his goodness, for the glory of the LORD lies in his goodness
God's grace and mercy free and sovereign, given of his own will to whom he pleases, his pardoning grace his chiefest glory

schools.summary: Gill: by his face is meant not his essence and very nature, which Moses must know he could never see, but the brightest displays of his grace and goodness in Christ, the fullest discoveries, which are too much for man in the present state to bear; the witnesses note God telling Moses he could not see his face, for no man could see God and live — the limit set to Moses' request, the unbearable fulness of God's glory to mortal man (the full sight of God more than flesh can bear: 'Thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live'; the face, 'thou canst not see my face,' meaning not his essence, his very nature, that Moses must know he could never see, but the brightest displays of his grace and goodness, the fullest discoveries, which are too much for man, in the present state, to bear; the reason, 'there shall no man see me, and live,' that the full and immediate sight of God's glory would overwhelm and consume mortal man; the meaning the commentators draw, that, while God grants much, he grants according to our measure, the full vision being reserved for the state of glory; that man in the flesh cannot bear the unveiled glory of God; that the sight of God face to face is the hope of the life to come, not of this).

themes: And he said, Thou canst not see my face
for there shall no man see me, and live
God setting a limit, that Moses could not see his face, no man seeing him and living
not the essence but the brightest displays of his glory, too much for mortal man; the full vision reserved for the state of glory

schools.summary: Gill: there was a place by him, near him, upon the rock where it had been for many days, near to which there was a fit place for Moses to have that view of the goodness and glory of God; the witnesses note God appointing Moses a place near him, to stand upon a rock — the providing of a place and a standing for the vision, near God and upon the rock (God himself setting the place where Moses shall stand: 'Behold, there is a place by me, and thou shalt stand upon a rock'; the place, 'there is a place by me,' near him, a place God himself appointed close to his presence, fit for Moses to have his view of the divine glory; the standing, 'thou shalt stand upon a rock,' that Moses was to stand upon the rock where God's presence had been, a sure and firm standing; the type the commentators draw, that the rock on which Moses stood to see God's glory shadows Christ, the Rock, on whom alone a man may stand to behold the glory and goodness of God; that there is no safe standing to see God but on the Rock; that God appoints both the place and the standing for the soul that would see his glory).

debates.question: What does the rock on which Moses stood to see God's glory shadow?

sides.position: Christ, the Rock — on whom alone a man may stand to behold the glory and goodness of God; there being no safe standing to see God but on the Rock

themes: And the LORD said, Behold, there is a place by me
and thou shalt stand upon a rock
God appointing Moses a place near him and a standing upon a rock for the vision of his glory
the rock shadowing Christ, the Rock, on whom alone a man may stand to behold the glory and goodness of God

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: with Kalisch, that the mysteriousness of this obscure section attains its highest climax in the three last verses, human language straining to express the unutterable; the witnesses note God promising to put Moses in a cleft of the rock and cover him with his hand while his glory passed by — the sheltering of Moses in the rock and under God's hand from the overwhelming glory (God hiding his servant while his glory goes by: 'while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a clift of the rock, and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by'; the passing, 'while my glory passeth by,' that the displays of his grace and goodness are made in passing; the hiding, 'I will put thee in a clift of the rock,' that Moses was sheltered in a cleft of the rock, hidden from the full blaze of the glory; the covering, 'will cover thee with my hand while I pass by,' that God's own hand covered Moses, shielding him from what he could not bear; the great type the commentators draw, that the cleft of the rock, in which Moses was hidden and covered, shadows Christ, the cleft Rock, in whom the believer is hidden and sheltered, safe to behold God's glory; the awe the commentators own, that human language strains here, the matter being too deep and mysterious for full utterance; that only in the Rock, covered by God's hand, may a man stand before his glory).

debates.question: What does the cleft of the rock, in which Moses was hidden, shadow?

sides.position: Christ, the cleft Rock — in whom the believer is hidden and sheltered, safe to behold God's glory; only in the Rock, covered by God's hand, may a man stand before his glory

themes: while my glory passeth by, that I will put thee in a clift of the rock
and will cover thee with my hand while I pass by
God hiding Moses in a cleft of the rock and covering him with his hand from the overwhelming glory
the cleft of the rock shadowing Christ, the cleft Rock, in whom the believer is hidden, safe to behold God's glory

schools.summary: Gill: as being covered with the hand may signify the obscurity of the former dispensation, the taking of it away may denote a clearer revelation of the grace and goodness of God in Christ under the Gospel, yet what is seen now compared to the future is but the back parts; the witnesses note God promising to take away his hand, that Moses should see his back parts, but his face should not be seen — the partial and gracious vision granted, the back parts and not the face (God showing what man can bear, not the full glory: 'I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts: but my face shall not be seen'; the uncovering, 'I will take away mine hand,' that, the glory passed, God would remove his covering hand; the sight, 'thou shalt see my back parts,' not the full face, but the after-glory, what could be seen and borne; the limit, 'my face shall not be seen,' the full, immediate glory still withheld; the meaning the commentators draw, that the covering hand may signify the obscurity of the former dispensation, and its taking away a more clear revelation of the grace and goodness of God in Christ under the Gospel, yet even this, compared to the glory to come, is but the back parts; that all our present sight of God, even the clearest in Christ, is partial, the full vision reserved for heaven; that we now see in part, but then face to face).

debates.question: What does seeing God's 'back parts' but not his face signify?

sides.position: The partial vision man can bear, not the full glory — the covering hand signifying the obscurity of the former dispensation, its taking away a clearer revelation of God's grace in Christ under the Gospel, yet even this, compared to the glory to come, but the back parts; all our present sight of God partial, the full vision reserved for heaven (1 Corinthians 13:12)

themes: And I will take away mine hand, and thou shalt see my back parts
but my face shall not be seen
God granting Moses to see his back parts but not his face, the partial vision man can bear
the clearer revelation of God's grace in Christ under the Gospel, yet but the back parts; the full vision reserved for heaven
